content:
First note that 7·11·13=1001.  We are looking for positive n<2018 with

 n²≡r (mod 7),  
 n²≡r (mod 11),  
 n²≡r (mod 13),

all giving the same integer r.  Since r∈{0,…,6} (it must lie below the smallest modulus, 7), we must have

 n²≡r (mod 1001),

with r=0,1,4 (these are exactly the r≤6 which are simultaneous quadratic residues mod 7,11,13).  For each r the number of solutions mod 1001 is

 f(r)=f₇(r)·f₁₁(r)·f₁₃(r),

where for a prime p, f_p(0)=1, f_p(r)=2 if r≠0 is a QR mod p, and f_p(r)=0 otherwise.  A quick check shows

 r=0: f₇=1, f₁₁=1, f₁₃=1 ⇒1 solution  
 r=1: f₇=2, f₁₁=2, f₁₃=2 ⇒8 solutions  
 r=4: f₇=2, f₁₁=2, f₁₃=2 ⇒8 solutions  

so in total there are S=1+8+8=17 residue‐classes mod 1001.

Next, n runs from 1 to 2017=2·1001+15, so each class c mod 1001 contributes

– 1 solution at n=1001+c,  
– 1 solution at n=c (if c≠0),  
– 1 more at n=2002+c (if 0≤c≤15).

Hence the count for each class c is
  N(c)=1+[c≠0]+[0≤c≤15].

Among our 17 solution‐classes exactly three lie in 0≤c≤15 (namely c=0,1,2), the other 14 are ≥16.  Thus

  Total = ∑₍classes₎N(c)
        =17·1 +16·1 +3·1
        =36.

Answer:  \boxed{36}.
